summary:
The thinner mix of a gypsum-bonded investment leads to greater setting expansion because it has lower mass and faster hydration kinetics, causing more pronounced volume change than thicker mixes. This effect arises from enhanced heat dissipation and higher surface-to-volume ratio accelerating early-stage reactions and inducing microstructural strain.  

(Note: Only include content strictly related to the core clinical point.)

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1's reasoning only—strictly focusing on what matters clinically regarding this specific dental investing scenario.

First sentence must capture why thinner mix causes different dimensional outcome—the key mechanism being setting expansion via physical/chemical factors tied to mixing consistency and geometry.

Second sentence should explain *how* those mechanisms operate within real-world dentistry context without adding external details beyond the original query scope.

I'll avoid mentioning unrelated topics such as "plaster splints", "cool water," or non-dental applications since they're tangential to actual clinical practice when placing crowns or bridges using investable materials.

Only retain facts directly linked to variation between thin/thick mixes impacting final restoration fit—a central concern clinicians face daily.
